distinction.theorem_formal: A WFF φ is a theorem ⊢φ iff there exists a valid proof of φ from the axioms.
distinction.theorem_informal: A claim is a 'theorem' in the colloquial sense if it is argued for and regarded as proven.
distinction.question: Are the theory's major conclusions theorems (formal) or argued-conclusions (informal)?

candidate_theorems.claim: Liberty = individual property rights over body and legitimately acquired assets.
candidate_theorems.derivable_formally: N/A — definitions are not proven, they are stipulated

candidate_theorems.claim: Denial of free will is self-refuting.
missing_for_formal_proof: Formal statement of the performative-contradiction rule as an inference rule
candidate_theorems.theorem_prover_difficulty: MEDIUM — achievable in Lean/Coq with proper definitions

candidate_theorems.claim: Tawhid implies prohibition on human-servitude institutions.
missing_for_formal_proof: Bridge premise: Tawhid (no God-concept without absolute-servitude-exclusion) → operational anti-human-servitude
Formal definition of 'servitude institution' vs. 'voluntary service'
candidate_theorems.theorem_prover_difficulty: HIGH — the bridge premise requires philosophical work

candidate_theorems.claim: Without unknown horizon, rational agents defect in last period.
candidate_theorems.note: This is a well-established theorem in game theory (Backward Induction Theorem for finitely repeated games). It is NOT derived within the Theory of Liberty — it is cited from external game theory. The theory USES this theorem but does not prove it. A formal system that imports theorems from external systems must either re-prove them or explicitly designate them as axioms.
missing_for_formal_proof: Must be either proven from theory's axioms or imported via explicit axiom
candidate_theorems.theorem_prover_difficulty: LOW for game theory — HIGH for embedding in this system

candidate_theorems.claim: Resurrection stabilizes liberty by making terminal period unknowable.
missing_for_formal_proof: Formal game-theoretic embedding (T-004 must be resolved first)
Formal definition: 'afterlife continuation' → 'T is unknowable to rational agent'

candidate_theorems.claim: A Consistent Formal System for liberty is equivalent to authentic religion.
candidate_theorems.note: Right-to-left (R → CFS): true by definition of 'authentic religion' = CFS for liberty. Left-to-right (CFS → R): requires formal proof that any CFS satisfying liberty axioms has the structural properties of authentic religion. This is the biconditional. The right-to-left is definitional (not a theorem). The left-to-right is a genuine theorem-candidate but requires the uniqueness proof first established as needed in Phase 2.
candidate_theorems.theorem_prover_difficulty: VERY HIGH — requires uniqueness proof as sub-theorem

candidate_theorems.claim: The state (monopoly coercion) is structurally impermissible.
missing_for_formal_proof: Formal definition of 'monopoly coercion' in deontic logic

candidate_theorems.claim: Libertarian free will is the only consistent starting axiom for normative systems.
candidate_theorems.note: The performative contradiction proves FW is PRESUPPOSED. Not that it is the ONLY starting axiom.
missing_for_formal_proof: Uniqueness proof: that no other starting axiom avoids the performative contradiction

verdict: The theory has 7 genuine candidate theorems and 1 definitional claim. NONE are currently formal theorems (no proof calculus exists). 5 of 7 could be formalized with moderate technical work (bridge premises + inference rule statements). 2 require prior uniqueness proof. Score 45/100: strong theorem candidates, zero formal proofs.
